sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, ACTION7, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 246, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1. Pink 6x6 character at left edge of a dark room. Three 4x4 colored blocks on the right wall: red (top, row 19-22), blue (middle, row 25-28), green/N (bottom, row 31-34). A white tile path extends right from the character into the room. Bottom HUD bar shows order: pink, red, green, blue.
- Goal model: Visit/touch the colored blocks in the order shown on the bottom bar: red, then green, then blue.
- Action model: RIGHT/LEFT/UP/DOWN move the character. Need to probe movement.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to enter the room, then navigate to the red block first.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 245 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 4.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so the second UP from 30-35 only changed the HUD bar at row 53 (the gray/dark gray tiles there). The character stayed at 30-35, blocks stayed at same positions. So the second UP was just advancing the HUD bar, not changing gameplay.
Wait, but the result said gameplay_changed=True, no_impact=False. But the only changes were at row 53, which is the HUD bar. This is confusing. Maybe the HUD detection is imperfect and sometimes misclassifies HUD changes as gameplay changes.
OK so I'm stuck. The character is at 30-35, blocks are at R=13-16, b=19-22, N=25-28. I can't reach 24-29 because green at 25-28 blocks it. I can't reach 18-23 or 12-17 either because I'd need to pass through 24-29 first.
Wait, but initially the blocks were at R=19, b=25, N=31. The character was at 36-41. I shifted blocks up by using ACTION7 from 24-29 (overlapping with green). Now blocks are at R=13, b=19, N=25.
What if I need to shift blocks DOWN? Is there a way to do that?
Or what if I need to reset the level?
Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. What if the goal is NOT to rearrange blocks but to do something else entirely? Let me look at the bottom bar again:
- Row 57: ccccccccccccccccccccMWWWWMcRRRRccNNNNccbbbbccccccccccccccccccccc
- Row 58: ccccccccccccccccccccMWMMWwgRRRRwgNNNNwgbbbbwcccccccccccccccccccc
- Row 59: ccccccccccccccccccccMWMMWgwRRRRgwNNNNgwbbbbgcccccccccccccccccccc
- Row 60: ccccccccccccccccccccMWWWWMcRRRRccNNNNccbbbbccccccccccccccccccccc
The bottom bar shows: pink (character), red, green, blue. Each is a 6x6 icon with a colored center.
What if the bottom bar is NOT the target order but rather a palette of available colors? And the goal is to use the character to "paint" something?
Or what if the bottom bar shows the ORDER in which blocks should be on the right wall? Currently: red (top), blue (middle), green (bottom). Target: red (top), green (middle), blue (bottom). So I need to swap blue and green.
Hmm, but I can't reach the blocks to swap them. Unless...
Wait, what if I need to use the path to interact with blocks? The path is at rows (char_row+2) to (char_row+3). When the character is at 30-35, the path is at 32-33. The blocks are at 13-16, 19-22, 25-28. The path doesn't overlap with any block.
But what if I extend the path RIGHT, and it "grabs" the nearest block? Let me try: from 30-35, press RIGHT multiple times to extend the path all the way to the blocks.
Actually wait, I already know RIGHT extends the path horizontally. The path is at 32-33, and the blocks are at cols 43-46 (right wall). So extending the path would make it reach the blocks horizontally, but the path is at rows 32-33 and the blocks are at rows 13-16, 19-22, 25-28. They don't overlap vertically.
Hmm, what if the path needs to be at the SAME row as a block to interact with it? Like, if I could move the path vertically...
Actually, wait. What if I need to move the character to a position where the path aligns with a block, THEN extend the path? For example:
- To interact with green at 25-28, character needs to be at 24-29 (path at 26-27, which overlaps with green 25-28)
- But I can't reach 24-29 because green blocks it
This is a chicken-and-egg problem. I need to reach 24-29 to interact with green, but green blocks 24-29.
Unless... what if I can move green out of the way first? How?
Wait, what if I can use the path to "push" blocks? Like, if I extend the path to overlap with a block, the block moves?
Let me try: from 30-35, extend the path RIGHT until it reaches the blocks. Even though the path is at 32-33 and blocks are at 25-28, maybe the path can still interact with them.
Actually, I just realized: the blocks are at cols 43-46 (right wall). The path starts at col 16 and extends right. So the path would need to extend to col 43 to reach the blocks. Let me try extending the path all the way.
But wait, I already tried RIGHT from 30-35 earlier, and it extended the path. Let me check how far the path extended.
Actually, I think I need to take a completely different approach. Let me try to understand the game by looking at the INITIAL state more carefully and figuring out what the first few moves should be.
Initial state:
- Character: 36-41
- Blocks: R=19-22, b=25-28, N=31-34
- Path: 38-39
From 36-41, the path is at 38-39. The blocks are at 19-22, 25-28, 31-34. The path doesn't overlap with any block.
What if the first move should be UP to 30-35? From 30-35, the path is at 32-33, which overlaps with green at 31-34. Then I can extend the path to interact with green.
But I already shifted blocks up by using ACTION7 from 24-29. Now blocks are at R=13, b=19, N=25, and I can't reach them.
I think I messed up the game state. Let me see if there's a way to reset or if I need to work with the current state.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The initial blocks were at R=19, b=25, N=31. After one ACTION7 from overlapping position, blocks shifted up by 6 rows: R=13, b=19, N=25. What if I use ACTION7 again from an overlapping position? Blocks would shift up again: R=7, b=13, N=19.
But that makes things worse, not better. I need blocks to shift DOWN, not up.
Hmm, what if there's a way to shift blocks down? What if I move the character to a position BELOW the blocks and use ACTION7?
Currently, blocks are at R=13-16, b=19-22, N=25-28. The character is at 30-35. The character is below all blocks. What if I use ACTION7 from 30-35?
I already know ACTION7 from 30-35 (no overlap) toggles to 36-41. But what if I first move to a position that overlaps with a block, then use ACTION7?
Wait, from 30-35, the character overlaps with... nothing. The blocks are at 13-16, 19-22, 25-28, and the character is at 30-35. No overlap.
From 36-41, the character overlaps with... nothing. Same situation.
So I can't use ACTION7 to shift blocks because I can't reach a position that overlaps with any block.
OK, I think I really messed up. Let me see if there's any way to recover.
Actually, wait. What if I can move the character to 24-29 by some other means? What if there's a way to "jump" over the green block?
Or what if the green block at 25-28 doesn't actually block movement to 24-29? Let me try UP from 30-35 one more time and carefully check the result.
Actually, I already tried UP from 30-35 and it had no_impact=True (only changed HUD). So the character can't move to 24-29.
Hmm, what if I need to use a different action? Like, what if MOUSE on the green block moves it?
Let me try MOUSE on the green block at row 26, col 44.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# I'm stuck. Let me try MOUSE on the green block to see if it moves it
# Green block is at rows 25-28, cols ~43-46

action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 26, 'col': 44}])
print(f"After MOUSE on green block:")
print(f"  gameplay_changed: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
blocks = [(n['color'], min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in seg if n['color'] in 'RbN' and n['pixels'] >= 10 and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
print(f"  Blocks: {blocks}")

# Also try MOUSE on the red block in the bottom bar
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 26}])
print(f"\nAfter MOUSE on bottom red:")
print(f"  gameplay_changed: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

# Try MOUSE on the green block in the bottom bar
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 32}])
print(f"\nAfter MOUSE on bottom green:")
print(f"  gameplay_changed: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

# Try MOUSE on the blue block in the bottom bar
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 38}])
print(f"\nAfter MOUSE on bottom blue:")
print(f"  gameplay_changed: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

blocks = [(n['color'], min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in seg if n['color'] in 'RbN' and n['pixels'] >= 10 and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
print(f"  Blocks: {blocks}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
